title: How to Work with Loneliness That Surfaces During Solo Journeys
body:
How to Work with Loneliness That Surfaces During Solo Journeys

You're out there, alone with your thoughts. Maybe you're on a solo psychedelic journey or just sitting quietly with yourself in some remote spot. The kind of loneliness that shows up then? It’s not the “I’m bored” or “I need to call someone” kind of loneliness. It’s raw. Sometimes it feels like a sharp ache in your chest. Other times, a dull, humming background noise you can’t quite shake. You might even feel like you’re falling into a deeper isolation, like no one else can get what you’re going through. Right?!

Here's the thing, loneliness during solo journeys isn’t a bug. It’s part of the process. It’s a sign, a message from your psyche, from your deeper self, that something needs your attention. You can’t just push it away or pretend it’s not there. That only makes it worse. So, how do you work with it? How do you sit with that ache without losing your mind or rushing back to distractions?

Recognize Loneliness as a Visitor, Not a Permanent Resident

Loneliness shows up like an uninvited guest. Sometimes it barges in, other times it sneaks quietly through the back door. But here's the truth: it doesn’t have to stay. When you notice it, say to yourself, “Hello, loneliness. I see you.” Don’t immediately try to fix it or run away. Just notice it. Feel it. You might find it’s holding onto a story you tell yourself, like “I’m completely alone” or “No one understands me.”

Try to catch those stories without judging them. This isn’t the time to argue or convince yourself otherwise. Simply observe. Imagine your loneliness as a cloud passing through your mental sky. It changes shape. It moves. It fades. It always does.

Ground Yourself Through Your Senses

When loneliness feels overwhelming, your mind might start spinning wild tales. To pull yourself back, reconnect with your body and senses. This is especially helpful during a solo psychedelic or spiritual journey when your mind is prone to wandering. Here’s a simple exercise:

  • Find a spot to sit comfortably, feet on the ground.
  • Close your eyes if that feels safe, and take three slow, deep breaths. Feel the air moving in and out.
  • Open your eyes and name five things you see around you.
  • Name four things you can touch - maybe the texture of your shirt, the warmth of the sun, the coolness of a table.
  • Name three sounds you hear.
  • Name two smells.
  • Name one taste, even if it’s just the aftertaste of your last meal.

This simple grounding pulls you away from spiraling thoughts and into the present moment. It reminds you that you’re alive, here, and connected to the world around you - even if you feel lonely.

Write It Out - No Holds Barred

One of the most concrete ways to work through that loneliness is with a pen and paper. Don’t overthink it. Just start writing what comes up. Maybe it’s a letter to your loneliness, or a journal entry about what you’re feeling right now. Don’t censor yourself. Let the words flow. If you’d like a helpful tool, I recommend a guided meditation journal (paid link). It’s designed to help you explore your inner space gently but with intention.

When you write, you make the invisible visible. You create a container for your feelings. This helps you to see patterns, triggers, and maybe even moments of relief. Writing can soothe that ache in a way nothing else will.

Reach Out When You’re Ready

Loneliness might whisper, “You’re completely alone.” But truth is, you’re not. There are others out there who’ve felt exactly what you’re feeling. Sometimes, the hardest part is asking for connection. Maybe you text a close friend, join an online community, or attend a local support group. It’s okay to be scared or reluctant. You don’t have to share everything or even anything at first. Just being around other humans can shift that loneliness.

For those doing psychedelic work, integrating after your journey is essential. I’ve seen people like Sarah, a woman in her early 30s, feel utterly lost after a solo ayahuasca ceremony. She reached out to a community integration circle and found, shockingly, that many shared her exact feelings of loneliness and confusion. That connection helped her feel less isolated and more anchored.

Use Technology to Track and Understand Your Inner State

Sounds weird, right? But hear me out. When loneliness hits, your nervous system is often dysregulated. Heart rate variability (HRV) can give you a window into what’s happening inside. I recommend a WHOOP HRV monitor (paid link) for this. It’s not just a fitness tracker; it helps you see when your body is stressed, when it recovers, and when it’s ready for connection or rest.

Knowing your body’s signals can help you decide whether to push through loneliness by engaging with others or to retreat gently into self-care. It’s a bit like having a dashboard for your emotional health.

Lean Into the Learning - Don’t Flee the Feeling

Loneliness isn’t just a feeling to escape. It’s a teacher. Yes, it sucks sometimes. But if you run from it every time, you miss what it’s trying to show you. What stories do you believe about yourself when you feel lonely? What parts of you have been ignored or shut down? What needs healing or attention?

One book that’s helped many people understand these inner landscapes is How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). It’s not just about psychedelics; it’s about how these experiences can shift your relationship with yourself and others, including how you experience loneliness.

Here’s a practical way to lean in. When the loneliness comes, instead of asking, “Why me?” try asking, “What are you trying to teach me?” Write down the answers. Sometimes they’ll surprise you.

Remember, You’re Not Alone in Feeling Alone

This might sound paradoxical, but many people who take solo journeys report loneliness at some point - even those with busy social lives. That’s because solo time strips away distractions. It leaves you face-to-face with your own mind and emotions. That can be terrifying but also deeply clarifying.

For some, like my friend David who spent months in silent retreat, loneliness morphed into a kind of spaciousness. For others, it’s a signal that they need to reconnect with their community or seek professional support. Know what I mean?

It’s okay to ask for help. There’s no shame in feeling lonely. It’s human. The myth that we have to be “strong” and “self-sufficient” all the time only isolates us further. If you want a compassionate place to start, check out my article on integrating psychedelic experiences, where I break down practical steps for coming back from solo journeys.

Be Gentle With Yourself, Always

Loneliness during solo journeys can feel like a test. But it’s really a call for kindness. Treat yourself like you would a close friend. If you wouldn’t say it to them, don’t say it to yourself. When you catch yourself spiraling into self-criticism or panic, try this:

  • Place your hand over your heart.
  • Take a deep breath.
  • Say silently or out loud: “I’m here. I see you. You’re safe.”

This simple self-compassion practice rewires your brain over time. It reminds your nervous system that you’re not a threat, even when loneliness feels like it’s swallowing you whole.

When to Seek Professional Help

If loneliness feels unbearable or leads to thoughts of harming yourself, please reach out for professional help immediately. There’s no shame in this. You’re not weak. You’re human. Here’s a trusted resource from Johns Hopkins Medicine on loneliness and mental health. They offer solid guidance and connections to care.

Sometimes, solo journeys uncover wounds that need more than self-work. Therapists trained in psychedelic integration or trauma can be lifesavers. Don’t hesitate to ask for help. You deserve support.

Final Thoughts: Loneliness Is Part of the Journey, Not the Destination

Look, I get it. Loneliness hurts. It can feel endless. But remember this: you’re on a path of discovery. The loneliness that surfaces during solo journeys is a sign that you’re peeling back layers, facing parts of yourself that have been ignored or hidden.

There’s no rush to “fix” it. The way through loneliness is often through patience, presence, and honest self-reflection. You’re never truly alone, even when it feels like it. There’s a whole web of connection around you, within you, waiting to be felt.

If you want to keep exploring these themes, head over to my site at kalesh.love. I share tools, writings, and community that can help you walk this path with more ease.

And if you’re curious about the altered states that often bring loneliness to the surface, check out Stealing Fire (paid link). It dives into the science and stories behind flow states, psychedelics, and consciousness shifts.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
